If we're looking to avoid a business that is in decline, what are the trends that can warn us ahead of time? Typically, we'll see the trend of both return on capital employed (ROCE) declining and this usually coincides with a decreasing amount of capital employed. This indicates the company is producing less profit from its investments and its total assets are decreasing. So after glancing at the trends within Universal Entertainment (TSE:6425), we weren't too hopeful.

Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. To calculate this metric for Universal Entertainment, this is the formula:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.00048 = JP¥266m ÷ (JP¥590b - JP¥36b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Thus, Universal Entertainment has an ROCE of 0.05%. In absolute terms, that's a low return and it also under-performs the Leisure industry average of 12%.

What Does the ROCE Trend For Universal Entertainment Tell Us?

In terms of Universal Entertainment's historical ROCE movements, the trend doesn't inspire confidence. Unfortunately the returns on capital have diminished from the 1.3% that they were earning five years ago. And on the capital employed front, the business is utilizing roughly the same amount of capital as it was back then. Since returns are falling and the business has the same amount of assets employed, this can suggest it's a mature business that hasn't had much growth in the last five years. If these trends continue, we wouldn't expect Universal Entertainment to turn into a multi-bagger.

data.title: Samjin LND (KOSDAQ:054090) Has Debt But No Earnings; Should You Worry?
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' When we think about how risky a company is, we always like to look at its use of debt, since debt overload can lead to ruin. We note that Samjin LND Co., Ltd. (KOSDAQ:054090) does have debt on its balance sheet. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt A Problem?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more usual (but still expensive) situation is where a company must dilute shareholders at a cheap share price simply to get debt under control. By replacing dilution, though, debt can be an extremely good tool for businesses that need capital to invest in growth at high rates of return. When we think about a company's use of debt, we first look at cash and debt together.

What Is Samjin LND's Debt?

You can click the graphic below for the historical numbers, but it shows that Samjin LND had ₩60.4b of debt in September 2025, down from ₩67.3b, one year before. However, it does have ₩28.5b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about ₩31.9b.

How Healthy Is Samjin LND's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Samjin LND had liabilities of ₩83.0b due within 12 months, and liabilities of ₩10.9b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting this, it had ₩28.5b in cash and ₩26.4b in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by ₩39.0b.

The deficiency here weighs heavily on the ₩16.3b company itself, as if a child were struggling under the weight of an enormous back-pack full of books, his sports gear, and a trumpet. So we'd watch its balance sheet closely, without a doubt. After all, Samjin LND would likely require a major re-capitalisation if it had to pay its creditors today. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But it is Samjin LND's earnings that will influence how the balance sheet holds up in the future. So when considering debt, it's definitely worth looking at the earnings trend. Click here for an interactive snapshot.

Check out our latest analysis for Samjin LND

Over 12 months, Samjin LND made a loss at the EBIT level, and saw its revenue drop to ₩145b, which is a fall of 19%. That's not what we would hope to see.

Caveat Emptor

Not only did Samjin LND's revenue slip over the last twelve months, but it also produced negative earnings before interest and tax (EBIT). Its EBIT loss was a whopping ₩5.7b. When we look at that alongside the significant liabilities, we're not particularly confident about the company. We'd want to see some strong near-term improvements before getting too interested in the stock. For example, we would not want to see a repeat of last year's loss of ₩19b. And until that time we think this is a risky stock. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. However, not all investment risk resides within the balance sheet - far from it. For instance, we've identified 2 warning signs for Samjin LND (1 makes us a bit uncomfortable) you should be aware of.

At the end of the day, it's often better to focus on companies that are free from net debt. You can access our special list of such companies (all with a track record of profit growth). It's free.

data.title: These 4 Measures Indicate That Fuji SeikiLtd (TSE:6400) Is Using Debt Reasonably Well
data.description:
Legendary fund manager Li Lu (who Charlie Munger backed) once said, 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. Importantly, Fuji Seiki Co.,Ltd. (TSE:6400) does carry debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. If things get really bad, the lenders can take control of the business. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

How Much Debt Does Fuji SeikiLtd Carry?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 Fuji SeikiLtd had debt of JP¥3.73b, up from JP¥3.33b in one year. However, it does have JP¥1.81b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about JP¥1.92b.

How Healthy Is Fuji SeikiLtd's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Fuji SeikiLtd had liabilities of JP¥3.78b due within 12 months, and liabilities of JP¥2.26b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of JP¥1.81b as well as receivables valued at JP¥1.76b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by JP¥2.48b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of JP¥2.35b, we think shareholders really should watch Fuji SeikiLtd's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

Check out our latest analysis for Fuji SeikiLtd

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). The advantage of this approach is that we take into account both the absolute quantum of debt (with net debt to EBITDA) and the actual interest expenses associated with that debt (with its interest cover ratio).

We'd say that Fuji SeikiLtd's moderate net debt to EBITDA ratio ( being 1.7), indicates prudence when it comes to debt. And its strong interest cover of 12.2 times, makes us even more comfortable. Also good is that Fuji SeikiLtd grew its EBIT at 18% over the last year, further increasing its ability to manage debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But you can't view debt in total isolation; since Fuji SeikiLtd will need earnings to service that debt. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So it's worth checking how much of that EBIT is backed by free cash flow. During the last three years, Fuji SeikiLtd generated free cash flow amounting to a very robust 90% of its EBIT, more than we'd expect. That puts it in a very strong position to pay down debt.

Our View

The good news is that Fuji SeikiLtd's demonstrated ability to cover its interest expense with its EBIT delights us like a fluffy puppy does a toddler. But the stark truth is that we are concerned by its level of total liabilities. Looking at all the aforementioned factors together, it strikes us that Fuji SeikiLtd can handle its debt fairly comfortably. On the plus side, this leverage can boost shareholder returns, but the potential downside is more risk of loss, so it's worth monitoring the balance sheet. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. data.title: Does YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372) Create Value For Shareholders?
data.description:
One of the best investments we can make is in our own knowledge and skill set. With that in mind, this article will work through how we can use Return On Equity (ROE) to better understand a business. We'll use ROE to examine YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372), by way of a worked example.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. In simpler terms, it measures the profitability of a company in relation to shareholder's equity.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Do You Calculate Return On Equity?

Return on equity can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for YMIRLINKInc is:

15% = JP¥451m ÷ JP¥2.9b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the profit over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every ¥1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of ¥0.15.

View our latest analysis for YMIRLINKInc

Does YMIRLINKInc Have A Good Return On Equity?

One simple way to determine if a company has a good return on equity is to compare it to the average for its industry. Importantly, this is far from a perfect measure, because companies differ significantly within the same industry classification. If you look at the image below, you can see YMIRLINKInc has a similar ROE to the average in the Software industry classification (14%).

That's neither particularly good, nor bad. While at least the ROE is not lower than the industry, its still worth checking what role the company's debt plays as high debt levels relative to equity may also make the ROE appear high. If true, then it is more an indication of risk than the potential.

The Importance Of Debt To Return On Equity

Most companies need money -- from somewhere -- to grow their profits. That cash can come from retained earnings, issuing new shares (equity), or debt. In the first and second cases, the ROE will reflect this use of cash for investment in the business. In the latter case, the use of debt will improve the returns, but will not change the equity. That will make the ROE look better than if no debt was used.

YMIRLINKInc's Debt And Its 15% ROE

One positive for shareholders is that YMIRLINKInc does not have any net debt! Its respectable ROE suggests it is a business worth watching, but it's even better the company achieved this without leverage. At the end of the day, when a company has zero debt, it is in a better position to take future growth opportunities.

Conclusion

Return on equity is useful for comparing the quality of different businesses. A company that can achieve a high return on equity without debt could be considered a high quality business. If two companies have around the same level of debt to equity, and one has a higher ROE, I'd generally prefer the one with higher ROE.

Having said that, while ROE is a useful indicator of business quality, you'll have to look at a whole range of factors to determine the right price to buy a stock. data.title: Returns On Capital Are A Standout For Timee (TSE:215A)
data.description:
If you're not sure where to start when looking for the next multi-bagger, there are a few key trends you should keep an eye out for. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. Speaking of which, we noticed some great changes in Timee's (TSE:215A) returns on capital, so let's have a look.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Timee:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.44 = JP¥6.7b ÷ (JP¥34b - JP¥18b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to October 2025).

So, Timee has an ROCE of 44%. That's a fantastic return and not only that, it outpaces the average of 20% earned by companies in a similar industry.

See our latest analysis for Timee

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Timee compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free analyst report for Timee .

The Trend Of ROCE

The trends we've noticed at Timee are quite reassuring. Over the last two years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 44%. The amount of capital employed has increased too, by 113%. The increasing returns on a growing amount of capital is common amongst multi-baggers and that's why we're impressed.

Another thing to note, Timee has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 55%. This can bring about some risks because the company is basically operating with a rather large reliance on its suppliers or other sorts of short-term creditors. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

The Bottom Line On Timee's ROCE

To sum it up, Timee has proven it can reinvest in the business and generate higher returns on that capital employed, which is terrific. Given the stock has declined 35% in the last year, this could be a good investment if the valuation and other metrics are also appealing. That being the case, research into the company's current valuation metrics and future prospects seems fitting.

Timee does have some risks, we noticed 2 warning signs (and 1 which doesn't sit too well with us) we think you should know about.

Timee is not the only stock earning high returns. If you'd like to see more, check out our free list of companies earning high returns on equity with solid fundamentals.

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) has wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of 548.3 million SEK and basic EPS of 0.04 SEK, setting the tone for a year where trailing 12 month revenue came in at 2.2 billion SEK and basic EPS was 1.49 SEK. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between 499.1 million SEK and 623.1 million SEK over the past year, with basic EPS ranging from 0.04 SEK to 0.74 SEK.   • On a trailing 12 month basis, Yubico earned net income of SEK128.1 million on SEK2.2b of revenue, which works out to a 5.8% net margin compared with 16% a year earlier.
  • Bears focus on that margin compression as a sign that hardware focused economics are under pressure, yet
    • they also assume in their own narrative that margins could reach 13.7% in about three years, which would be higher than the current 5.8% even though recent quarters include Q4 FY 2025 net income of just SEK3.7 million on SEK548.3 million of revenue, a relatively thin quarter.
    • this gap between today’s 5.8% trailing margin and the implied 13.7% margin view shows that even cautious analysts are anchoring their concerns to what happens from here, not to the latest reported profitability alone.

  • Quarterly basic EPS for FY 2025 moved from SEK0.59 in Q1 to SEK0.74 in Q3, then down to SEK0.04 in Q4, while trailing 12 month EPS fell from SEK4.32 in Q4 FY 2024 to SEK1.49 by Q4 FY 2025.
  • Bulls talk about earnings growing roughly 38.4% per year over the next few years and, in their scenario, profits rising from SEK254.5 million today to SEK719.2 million by around 2028, yet
    • the current trailing EPS of SEK1.49 and Q4 EPS of SEK0.04 show that, for now, earnings sit a long way below those bullish outcomes and have moved down from the SEK4.32 trailing EPS level seen a year earlier.
    • this contrast means anyone leaning on the bullish view is effectively saying the subscription shift and enterprise roll outs will need to lift earnings from today’s reset base, not from the higher profitability implied by last year’s trailing numbers.

    • at the same time, the gap between SEK54.08 and SEK134.33 on the DCF fair value, as well as the SEK85.00 analyst target, shows how much of the upside case depends on those growth forecasts materialising over the next few years.

data.title: Is Heineken (ENXTAM:HEIA) Pricing Look Interesting After Recent Share Price Strength
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Heineken at around €78 a share still offers value, the key question is how its current price compares with what the business might reasonably be worth.
  • The stock has recently been firm, with returns of 6.0% over the last 7 days, 14.4% over the last 30 days, 13.2% year to date, and 0.8% over the last year. However, the 3 year and 5 year returns of negative 12.9% and negative 1.0% show a much flatter longer term picture.
  • Recent news around Heineken has focused on its position as a major global brewer and how it is managing brand strength and operations in different markets. This helps frame how investors think about its long term prospects. These themes often influence how the market prices the shares, particularly when sentiment shifts between focusing on shorter term share price moves and longer term fundamentals.
  • Right now, Heineken scores 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks, giving it a valuation score of 3. Next we will look at what different valuation methods indicate about that number, before finishing with a way of thinking about value that can give you a fuller picture than any single model.

Find out why Heineken's 0.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Heineken Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future euros are worth in today’s money.

For Heineken, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €2.57b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2028, with Simply Wall St then extrapolating free cash flow out to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow for 2026 is €2.66b and for 2035 is €3.39b, with intermediate years gradually stepping up between those figures.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €150.96 per share. Compared with a recent share price around €78, this output suggests the stock is 48.3% undervalued according to this DCF model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Heineken is undervalued by 48.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 234 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Heineken.

Approach 2: Heineken Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Heineken, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they feel those earnings are.

Heineken currently trades on a P/E of 23.05x. That sits above the Beverage industry average of 17.72x, but slightly below the peer group average of 24.82x. On their own, these comparisons show where the market is pricing Heineken relative to its sector and closest competitors, but they do not fully reflect the company’s specific growth outlook, profitability or risk profile.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio tackles that by estimating what a company’s P/E might be given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and identified risks. For Heineken, this Fair Ratio is 21.36x, which is below the current 23.05x P/E. On this basis, the shares screens as somewhat expensive relative to what the Fair Ratio model suggests would be a more neutral level.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 100 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Heineken Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply the stories investors tell about a company that link its business outlook to a set of numbers like future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives to set your own view on Heineken, compare it with others, and see in real time how each view changes when new earnings or news arrive. For example, one Heineken Narrative might lean closer to a fair value around €64.62 with more cautious assumptions, while another leans toward €110.19 with more optimistic expectations. By comparing each Narrative’s Fair Value with today’s share price, you get a clear, easy to use framework for assessing whether the stock looks expensive, cheap or roughly in line with your own story.

For Heineken however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Heineken Narratives:

🐂 Heineken Bull Case

Fair value in this bull case: €85.16 per share

Implied discount to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 8.3% below the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 3.73% a year

  • Focuses on premium brands, emerging markets and low or no alcohol products as key drivers of revenue and margin strength.
  • Assumes gradual improvement in profit margins and steady earnings growth, supported by cost savings and digital efficiency programs.
  • Flags currency swings, reliance on emerging markets and pressure in mature European markets as the main things that could upset this story.

🐻 Heineken Bear Case

Fair value in this bear case: €77.50 per share

Implied premium to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 0.7% above the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 6.62% a year

  • Builds a valuation mainly from a DCF model using free cash flow projections over 5 years and two different terminal value methods.
  • Arrives at an intrinsic value range of €72 to €91 per share and then applies a 15% margin of safety, which pulls the working fair value down to about €77.50.
  • Highlights that at a share price just above €78, Heineken sits close to this cautious fair value, so any change in cash flow, discount rate or growth assumptions can quickly shift the margin of safety up or down.

data.title: Mapfre (BME:MAP) Margin Improvement To 4.8% Challenges Persistent Bearish Narratives
data.description:
Mapfre (BME:MAP) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €4.4b and net income of €303.6m, adding to a trailing twelve month performance that includes revenue of €23.5b and net income of €1.1b alongside EPS of €0.37. Over the past year the company has seen revenue move from €29.2b to €23.5b on a trailing basis, while net income has shifted from €967.5m to €1.1b and EPS has gone from €0.32 to €0.37. This sets up a results season in which investors will be weighing that earnings profile against revenue trends. With net profit margins running at 4.8% versus 3.3% a year earlier, the latest numbers put profitability in sharper focus as you think about the balance between growth and income.

See our full analysis for Mapfre.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to line these results up against the most widely held narratives about Mapfre to see which stories the numbers support and which ones they start to question.

See what the community is saying about Mapfre

Advertisement

Margins Firm Up With 4.8% Net Profit

  • On a trailing basis, Mapfre earned €1.1b on €23.5b of revenue, which works out to a 4.8% net profit margin compared with 3.3% a year earlier.
  • Supporters on the bullish side point to this higher margin as evidence that underwriting and cost control are moving in the right direction. However, the improvement sits alongside only moderate forecast earnings growth of 4.13% per year, which means:
    • the recent 17.1% earnings growth over the last year and 13.8% per year over five years look stronger than the forward growth assumptions tied to their thesis
    • investors who agree with the bullish view still need to decide how much weight to give the current 4.8% margin if future growth settles closer to those lower forecast rates

Bulls argue that the latest margin profile could be the start of a more profitable phase for Mapfre, not just a one off. 🐂 Mapfre Bull Case

P/E Of 10.4x Versus Peers At 13x

  • Mapfre trades on a trailing P/E of 10.4x, compared with a peer average of 13x and a European Insurance industry average of 12.8x, while the current share price of €3.82 sits well below the DCF fair value of about €7.90.
  • Critics in the bearish camp argue that exposure to slower growing markets, climate risks and pressure on investment returns justify a lower multiple. Yet the combination of a 4.8% net margin and 17.1% earnings growth over the last year creates tension with that view because:
    • those profitability and growth figures are being valued at a discount to both peers and the DCF fair value, which indicates the market is already pricing in a fair amount of caution
    • at the same time, forecast earnings growth of 4.13% per year and revenue growth of 3.5% per year sit below the wider Spanish market, which lines up with the bears’ concern about more modest expansion

Skeptics suggest the current discount could be justified if those lower growth forecasts play out, but the earnings track record tells a stronger story than a simple low P/E suggests. 🐻 Mapfre Bear Case

Dividend Yield At 4.31% Supports Income Angle

  • The trailing dividend yield is 4.31%, backed by trailing 12 month net income of €1.1b and earnings per share of €0.37, so recent payouts are coming from a business that has reported both profit and positive EPS.
  • The consensus narrative highlights Mapfre’s capital strength and diversification as key supports for steady earnings, and these results connect to that argument because:
    • net income has moved from €967.5m to €1.1b over the trailing period, which aligns with the view that earnings have held up while the company maintains a reported solvency ratio in the 200% area in the narrative
    • forecast revenue growth of 2.7% to 3.5% per year and stable earnings expectations around €1.1b sit comfortably with the idea of a business geared more toward income and resilience than rapid expansion

data.title: Raute Oyj (HLSE:RAUTE) Margin Resilience Challenges Cautious Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Raute Oyj (HLSE:RAUTE) closed out FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €36.2 million and basic EPS of €0.59, alongside net income of €3.6 million, setting a clear snapshot of how the year ended. Over the past few quarters, the company has seen revenue move from €51.9 million and EPS of €0.70 in Q1 2025 to €43.8 million with EPS of €0.20 in Q2, then €43.7 million with EPS of €0.66 in Q3 before landing at the latest Q4 figures, giving investors a full view of how the top and bottom line have tracked through the year. With trailing twelve month EPS at €2.12 and a net margin of 6.3% in the inputs, the focus is now on how durable those margins look relative to what the market expects next.

See our full analysis for Raute Oyj.

With the latest results on the table, the next step is to see how these numbers line up against the most common stories investors tell about Raute, and where the data pushes back on those narratives.

See what the community is saying about Raute Oyj

Advertisement

Margins Holding Up Around 6.3%

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Raute generated €12.9 million of net income on €175.7 million of revenue, which works out to a 6.3% net margin compared with 5.1% in the prior period mentioned in the inputs.
  • Analysts' consensus view expects margins to move higher even if revenue softens, which creates an interesting tension:
    • Forecasts in the inputs point to revenue declining about 1.4% per year over the next three years, yet margins are assumed to rise from 6.3% to 7.4%, so the story leans on Raute keeping more profit from each euro of sales.
    • Q4 2025 net income of €3.6 million on €36.2 million of revenue sits roughly in line with that 6.3% trailing margin, which supports the idea that recent profitability levels are already close to the margin profile analysts are building into their models.

Earnings Growth Versus Softer Revenue Trend

  • Over the last year in the dataset, earnings grew 25.3% while the trailing revenue line moved from €204.6 million at 2024 Q4 to €175.7 million at 2025 Q4, so profit growth has come alongside a softer top line in the recent figures provided.
  • Consensus narrative inputs highlight a more cautious path for both revenue and earnings, and the current numbers cut both ways:
    • On one hand, the company has a five year earnings growth rate of 57.6% per year and trailing EPS of €2.12, which supports the view that Raute can still generate healthy profit even when revenue is not expanding quickly.
    • On the other hand, forecasts in the data call for roughly 0.3% per year earnings decline and 1.4% per year revenue decline over the next three years, so the recent 25.3% earnings lift offers a higher bar that future results would need to match for this stronger earnings pattern to continue.
On a year where profit growth outpaced the top line, some investors are asking whether that pattern can last, or if the forecasted dip in revenue and earnings will start to bite into Raute's story, which is exactly what bulls and bears debate in more depth in the dedicated Raute narratives. 🐂 Raute Oyj Bull Case

Low P/E And DCF Gap To €23.65

  • Raute trades on a trailing P/E of 6.9x at a share price of €14.40, and the inputs include a DCF fair value of €23.65, so the modelled value is materially higher than where the stock is currently trading.
  • Bears focus on the softer outlook and cash flow coverage, and the valuation gap gives them specific numbers to point to:
    • The data flags that the 3.89% dividend yield is not well covered by free cash flow, so skeptics question how sustainable that payout is if revenue declines about 1.4% per year and earnings about 0.3% per year as forecasts suggest.
    • At the same time, Raute's 6.9x P/E sits below peer and European Machinery averages of 27.7x and 21.9x, which means the current pricing reflects more conservative expectations than the DCF fair value of €23.65, a point that challenges how far the bearish view can stretch purely on valuation.
With a low P/E, an uncovered dividend and forecasts pointing to softer revenue, skeptics argue the discount is earned, while the large DCF gap keeps the debate alive over whether the market is pricing in too much caution right now. 🐻 Raute Oyj Bear Case

data.title: Verisure (OM:VSURE) Q4 Loss Worsens To €121.3m Challenging Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Verisure (OM:VSURE) FY 2025 Earnings Snapshot

Verisure (OM:VSURE) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €964.7 million and a basic EPS loss of €0.12, while trailing 12 month figures show revenue of about €3.7 billion and a basic EPS loss of €0.30. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from €870.2 million in Q4 2024 to €964.7 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS stayed in loss making territory, shifting from a €0.07 loss per share in Q4 2024 to a €0.12 loss per share in Q4 2025. With revenue growth and ongoing losses sitting side by side, investors are likely to focus on how quickly margins can tighten toward break even and eventually support more durable profitability.

See our full analysis for Verisure.

With the headline numbers set, the next step is to see how this mix of revenue growth and continuing losses lines up with the widely shared narratives around Verisure, and where those stories might need an update.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

€3.7b revenue with €255.9m loss still points to margin pressure

  • Over the last 12 months Verisure generated about €3.7b in revenue and reported a net loss of €255.9m, which means the business is bringing in a lot of sales but costs are still high enough to keep it firmly in loss making territory.
  • What is interesting for a more optimistic take is that revenue grew 9.9% over the past year and forecasts in the data point to about 8.7% annual revenue growth, yet the trailing 12 month loss of €255.9m shows that the bullish idea of strong earnings growth still has to work through a cost base that has not produced profits so far.
    • This supports the bullish focus on growth because €3.7b in sales gives plenty of scale to work with if costs can be managed more tightly.
    • At the same time, the ongoing losses challenge any bullish view that expects quick earnings improvement without clear evidence of margin progress in the reported numbers.

Q4 loss of €121.3m stands out versus earlier quarters

  • In FY 2025, Q4 revenue of €964.7m came alongside a net loss of €121.3m, compared with losses of €47.8m in Q3 and €43.4m in each of Q2 and Q1, so the final quarter shows a much larger hit to the bottom line than the earlier part of the year.
  • Critics who lean bearish often point to the risk that losses widen instead of narrowing, and the step up from €47.8m loss in Q3 to €121.3m loss in Q4, even as revenue moved from €933m to €964.7m, gives that cautious view some backing.
    • The pattern where quarterly revenue sits in the €923.9m to €964.7m range while the latest quarter absorbs the deepest loss suggests costs or one off items may be biting harder at higher scale.
    • Because net income stayed negative in every reported quarter, the bearish concern that profitability has not yet turned the corner is reflected clearly in these FY 2025 figures.
On quarters like this, skeptics argue the path to profitability looks longer than bulls hope and that is exactly the kind of gap our bear case walks through in more detail. 🐻 Verisure Bear Case

Premium P/S of 2.9x versus industry 0.5x

  • The stock is trading on a P/S of 2.9x compared with an industry average of 0.5x and a peer average of 1.0x, while the current share price of €10.38 sits below both an 18.63 analyst price target and a €21.64 DCF fair value in the data.
  • Supporters of the bullish narrative argue that this kind of revenue growth and the forecast 63.5% yearly earnings improvement can justify paying more for each euro of sales, and the gap between €10.38 and the €21.64 DCF fair value is often used to back that view.
    • The premium P/S multiple against industry and peers shows the market is already pricing Verisure differently, which lines up with the bullish idea that its €3.7b revenue base and forecast growth deserve a higher tag.
    • However, the fact that the company is still loss making over the trailing 12 months means the bullish case hinges on those earnings forecasts turning into actual profits rather than remaining just estimates.
If you want to see how that premium valuation stacks up against the full range of possible stories for the business, the broader community view on the company can help you weigh both the upside case and the ongoing risks. 📊 Read the full Verisure Consensus Narrative.
